data.title: This Bitcoin Miner from Wall Street Plans 317% Hash Rate Jump by September
data.description:
HIVE Digital Technologies, the publicly listed Bitcoin miner from Wall Street (NASDAQ: HIVE), announced its January production results alongside a significant expansion into Paraguay.

Despite the overall slowdown in the industry and a decline in production reported by other miners, HIVE managed to maintain its levels from late 2024.

HIVE Digital Reports Strong January Performance

The blockchain infrastructure provider mined 102 Bitcoin in January while maintaining an average hashrate of 5.7 Exahash per second (EH/s). The company's Bitcoin holdings reached 2,657 BTC, valued at approximately $271 million at month-end, reflecting a 34% increase year-over-year.

HIVE's January operations maintained strong efficiency metrics, achieving an average daily production of 3.3 Bitcoin, equivalent to 18 BTC per Exahash. The company's mining fleet operated at 22 Joules per Terahash, with peak performance reaching 6 EH/s for Bitcoin-only mining and 6.4 EH/s for Bitcoin-equivalent hashrate.Aydin Kilic, President & CEO of HIVE

“We are targeting a blended fleet efficiency of 16.5 J/TH, positioning HIVE among the most energy-efficient Bitcoin miners globally,” commented Aydin Kilic, President and CEO of HIVE. “With consistent top industry rankings for uptime efficiency, we remain committed to operational excellence.”

At the end of 2024, the company announced its decision to move its headquarters from Vancouver, Canada, to San Antonio, Texas, signaling a strategic shift toward the U.S. market.

Miners Face Challenges in 2025

Compared to December 2024, HIVE mined just one fewer Bitcoin, which is a strong result relative to its competitors. This week, Finance Magnates reported that major U.S. Bitcoin miners experienced a drop in production in January, as weather-related disruptions and a rising hashrate impacted operations.

For example, Marathon Digital Holdings (NASDAQ: MARA), one of the largest miners in the industry, saw a 12% decline in monthly production, mining 750 Bitcoin in January compared to 865 in December.

Meanwhile, Hut 8 (NASDAQ: HUT) focused on infrastructure upgrades during the month, but its Bitcoin production fell to 65 BTC from 89 BTC the previous month.

HIVE Digital Technologies
@HIVEDigitalTech

HIVE’s January 2025 Bitcoin Production Update:

🟠 102 Bitcoin Mined in January

🟠 2,657 BTC in HODL Portfolio

Full details: https://t.co/86eZDVIK2l pic.twitter.com/9TPv9jVlHK

Feb 06, 2025

Paraguay Expansion

HIVE also unveiled plans to acquire Bitfarms' 200-megawatt hydro-powered Bitcoin mining facility in Paraguay. The acquisition is expected to catapult HIVE's global Bitcoin mining hashrate to 25 EH/s by September 2025, representing a 317% increase from current levels.Frank Holmes, Executive Chairman of HIVE

“This acquisition makes us one of Latin America’s largest Bitcoin mining operators,” Frank Holmes, Executive Chairman of HIVE, said. “We are deepening our relationship with Paraguay President Santiago Peña and key political leaders to support our growing presence there, which will bring mutual economic benefits for HIVE shareholders and the local community.”

The company estimates the total net cost to complete the Bitfarms site at $400,000 per megawatt. Upon completion, HIVE's total operational capacity is projected to reach approximately 430 MW by the third quarter of 2025.

The company also strengthened its leadership team by appointing Lieutenant General (Ret.) John R. Evans Jr. to its Global Strategy Advisory Committee.

data.title: Romania Retail Sales Growth at 5-Month High
data.description:
Retail sales in Romania advanced by 7.8% year-on-year in December 2024, easing from a downwardly revised 8.8% increase in the previous month.

This marked the softest expansion in retail activity since July, as sales slowed both for food, beverages, and tobacco (1% vs 3.7% in November) and automotive fuel (3.1% vs 3.4%).

Conversely, trade increased faster for non-food products (15.7% vs 14.9%).

On a monthly basis, retail trade fell by 1.3% in December, following a revised flat reading in the preceding period.

data.title: Mærsk's FY24 Profit, Revenue Grows
data.description:
A.P. Møller-Mærsk (MAERSK-A.CO, MAERSK-B.CO) said Thursday that its profit and revenue for the full-year 2024 grew year over year.

Profit attributable to the company for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was $6.11 billion, compared with $3.82 billion a year ago. EPS moved to $387 from $227.

The Danish shipping giant's revenue was $55.48 billion, compared with $51.07 billion earlier.

Meanwhile, the board proposed a dividend of 1,120 Danish kroner per share for the period, expected to be paid on March 21. It paid 515 kroner per share a year ago.

data.title: Trade War Between U.S. and China Could Weaken Eurozone Economy, Says ECB's Cipollone
data.description:
By Paul Hannon

A trade war between the U.S. and China could lead to weaker economic growth and falling inflation in the eurozone, a top official at the European Central Bank warned in an interview published Thursday.

Speaking to Reuters, ECB Executive Board member Piero Cipollone said high U.S. tariffs on imports from the world's second largest economy could push Chinese businesses to look for buyers elsewhere, and increase their competition with eurozone rivals.

"Trade barriers will force China to sell its goods elsewhere and the competition from China could be a serious threat to us," he said. "These goods showing up in Europe could have both a deflationary and a contractionary impact because they would crowd out local products."

The U.S. and China entered a new trade war on Tuesday, with tariffs of 10% going into effect on Chinese goods. Beijing struck back with 10%-15% levies that will take effect Feb. 10 on select American goods.

Write to Paul Hannon at paul.hannon@wsj.com

data.title: Compass Group's Revenue Rises on Sustained Growth Across All Divisions
data.description:
By Najat Kantouar

Compass Group reported a rise in revenue for the first quarter, reflecting growth across all divisions and continued strong client retention.

The London-listed catering contractor said Thursday that organic revenue for the three months ended Dec. 31 rose 9.2%.

In North America--which represents the company's biggest market--organic revenue increased 9.7%. Meanwhile, organic revenue was up 8.4% in Europe and 7.9% in the rest of the world.

Compass Group maintained its full-year guidance, which includes high single-digit underlying operating profit growth and organic revenue growth above 7.5%. It also anticipates continued margin progression.

Write to Najat Kantouar at najat.kantouar@wsj.com

data.title: Compass Fiscal Q1 Organic Revenue Up, Affirms Outlook
data.description:
Compass Group said Thursday its organic revenue for the fiscal first quarter ended Dec. 31, 2024, increased year over year.

The food services company's organic revenue rose 9.2% in the quarter, with upticks across all regions.

Compass maintained its fiscal 2025 guidance of delivering high single-digit underlying operating profit growth and organic revenue growth above 7.5%.

data.title: Warpaint London Pretax Profit, Revenue Rise
data.description:
By Anthony O. Goriainoff

Warpaint London said pretax profit and revenue rose last year and that its strong performance has continued into January despite continuing consumer spending headwinds.

The U.K. cosmetics company said Thursday that it expects to report 2024 pretax profit of around 24 million pounds ($30 million) compared with 18.1 million the year before on revenue that rose to around 102 million pounds from 89.6 million pounds.

The company said January revenue was 15% ahead of the year-prior period and at an improved margin.

"We expect to see continued growth across the group in 2025," Chief Executive Sam Bazini said.

Write to Anthony O. Goriainoff at anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com

data.description:

(07:12 GMT) *Anglo American Now Sees 2025 Nickel Production at 37 kt to 39 kt
(07:13 GMT) *Anglo American Had Seen 2025 Nickel Production at 35 kt 37 kt
(07:34 GMT) Anglo American Cuts 2025 Diamond View After Production Falls

By Adam Whittaker

Anglo American reported a fall in fourth-quarter copper and diamond production and cut its 2025 diamond production guidance.

The London-listed mining giant said Thursday that it dug out 198,000 tons of copper, 14% less than in the fourth quarter of 2023, due to the planned shut down of its smaller Los Bronces plant.

Diamond production also fell 26% on year to 5.8 million carats from 7.9 million carats as Anglo American cut production in response to lower demand and higher than normal levels of inventory.

Anglo American also cuts its full-year 2025 diamond production guidance to between 20 million to 23 million carats, down from previous guidance of 30 million to 33 million carats.

The company kept its full-year guidance for copper production for 2025 and 2026 unchanged.

Across the business, all division met their full-year production guidance, the company said.

Anglo American also said it is making "excellent" progress with its portfolio simplification. The sales process for its nickel business is well progressed, and its continue to prepare the De Beers business for separation, the company said.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Update: Market Chatter: Lloyds to Challenge Court Ruling in GBP1 Billion Tax Case Linked to Winddown of Irish Operations
data.description:
Lloyds Banking Group intends to appeal a tribunal's decision that it owes the UK 1 billion pounds sterling in back taxes over the winddown of its operations in Ireland more than 10 years ago, Bloomberg News reported Wednesday, citing the bank.

The dispute stemmed from the British lender's exit of its Irish operations in 2010. The bank believes it is entitled to 3.8 billion pounds in tax relief but the tribunal disagreed.

People familiar with the matter told Bloomberg that the case would be transferred to the upper tax tribunal and that executives at the bank would not expect a resolution for years.

Amid the latest development in the years-long dispute, Lloyds is expected to recognize a cash reduction in its earnings, a person familiar with the matter said, noting that the tribunal's decision will not have any capital impact on the bank.

A Lloyds spokesperson told MT Newswires, "We note the judgment of the First Tier Tax tribunal deciding against the Group's claim for group relief for losses incurred in its former Irish banking subsidiary, which ceased trading on 31 December 2010."

"Having reviewed the judgment in detail, we respectfully but fundamentally disagree with the Tribunal's decision and will be appealing the judgment. The Group is one of the UK's largest taxpayers, and is at all times committed to paying all the tax that it owes," the spokesperson added.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Dhruv Consultancy's Net Profit Slips in Fiscal Q3
data.description:
Dhruv Consultancy Services said its net profit dropped to 21.5 million Indian rupees in the fiscal third quarter from 27 million rupees a year ago, according to an Indian bourse filing on Wednesday.

Earnings per share during the quarter ended Dec. 31, 2024, slipped to 1.20 rupees from 1.69 rupees a year earlier.

Total revenue in fiscal Q3 fell year on year to 219.9 billion rupees from 226 billion rupees, according to the filing.

The company also declared an interim dividend of 0.10 rupees per share, according to the filing.

data.title: Al Ashghal Al Moysra Secures SAR17 Million Deal to Transport Equipment
data.description:
Al Ashghal Al Moysra (SASE:9608) secured a project worth 16.5 million Saudi riyals from the Royal Commission for Jubail and Yanb.

The logistics company's deal covers the transport of the client's furniture and office equipment, according to a Thursday release.

data.title: Nikkei Rises 0.6%, Led by Brokerage, Electronics Stocks — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks ended higher amid receding concerns of trade friction between the U.S. and its trading partners. Brokerage and electronics stocks led the gains. Nomura Holdings rose 3.8% after its nine-month net profit more than doubled. Renesas Electronics surged 13% after its 4Q results. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.6% to 39066.53. USD/JPY is at 152.64, compared with 152.60 as of Wednesday 5 p.m. Eastern time. Investors are closely watching for developments regarding U.S. tariffs. The 10-year Japanese government bond yield fell 2 basis points to 1.260%. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

data.title: Gold May Rise Further Near-Term on Tariff Risks, Robust Central Bank Demand — Market Talk
data.description: Gold may rise higher than previously forecast in the near term owing to tariff risks and robust central bank demand, Citi Research's Kenny Hu says. The precious metal has rallied 10% year-to-date, breaching Citi's up to three-month target of $2,800/oz,the analyst says in a research report. Citi raises this target to $3,000/oz while keeping its 6-12 month target of $3,000/oz unchanged. Gold's bull market appears set to persist with trade wars and geopolitical tensions reinforcing the reserve diversification and de-dollarization trend and supporting emerging-market central banks' demand, the analyst adds. Spot gold is 0.2% lower at $2,861.07/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Ester Industries Swings to Profit in Fiscal Q3
data.description:
Ester Industries posted a consolidated net profit of 248.2 million Indian rupees in the fiscal third quarter, swinging from a consolidated loss of 448.2 million rupees a year ago, according to an Indian bourse filing on Wednesday by the polyester chips manufacturer.

Earnings per share during the quarter ended Dec. 31, 2024, stood at 2.64 rupees, compared with a loss per share of 5.37 rupees a year earlier.

Revenue from operations in fiscal Q3 increased year on year to 3.46 billion rupees from 2.68 billion rupees, according to the filing.

data.title: AstraZeneca Expects Further Sales, Earnings Growth After Results Beat Market Views
data.description:
By Helena Smolak

AstraZeneca anticipates further sales and earnings growth this year after its fourth-quarter results topped analysts' expectations, boosted by high demand for its key drugs that offset a slight sales drop in one of its key markets China.

The British pharmaceuticals major on Thursday said total revenue is expected to increase at constant exchange rates by a high single-digit percentage in 2025, with core earnings per share growth at a low double-digit percentage.

Core earnings per share--its preferred metric, which strips out exceptional and other one-off items--were $2.09 for the three months ended Dec. 31, up from $1.45 for the comparable period a year earlier and outpacing estimates of $2.07 taken from a company-compiled consensus.

Total revenue for the quarter was $14.89 billion, ahead of expectations of $14.2 billion, and up from $12.02 billion the year before.

Write to Helena Smolak at helena.smolak@wsj.com

data.title: Nokia to Modernize Vietnam Air Traffic Control
data.description:
Nokia will upgrade Vietnam Air Traffic Management Corp.'s air traffic management system to boost its performance, flexibility and safety.

The Finnish telecommunications group and prime contractor Advanced Network System Vietnam are scheduled to roll out Nokia's Internet Protocol/Multi-Protocol Label Switching system and Quantum-Safe Network in the second quarter of 2025, replacing the legacy Synchronous Digital Hierarchy system, according to a Wednesday release.

data.title: A.P Moller-Maersk Swings to Profit as Red Sea Shipping Disruptions Boost Rates
data.description:
(07:04 GMT) *A.P Moller-Maersk 2024 Rev $55.48B
(07:06 GMT) *A.P Moller-Maersk 2024 Net Pft $6.11B
(07:11 GMT) *A.P Moller-Maersk 4Q Rev $14.59B
(07:11 GMT) *A.P Moller-Maersk 4Q EBITDA $3.6B
(07:11 GMT) *A.P Moller-Maersk 4Q EBIT $2.05B
(07:14 GMT) *A.P Moller-Maersk 4Q Net Pft $2.085B
(07:14 GMT) *Analysts Had A.P Moller-Maersk 4Q Net Pft at $1.31B
(07:14 GMT) *Analysts Had A.P Moller-Maersk 4Q Rev at $13.89B
(07:28 GMT) A.P Moller-Maersk Swings to Profit as Red Sea Shipping Disruptions Boost Rates

By Pierre Bertrand

A.P. Moeller-Maersk swung to a net profit in the fourth quarter that exceeded analysts' expectations, as disruptions to the Red Sea shipping supported higher shipping rates.

The Danish shipping company said it made $2.085 billion in net profit for the quarter, compared with a net loss of $436 million a year ago, when an oversupply of cargo capacity weighed on freight rates.

Quarterly revenue came to $14.59 billion, a 24% on-year increase as continued disruption to shipping lanes in the Red Sea pushed up shipping rates by 38% in the quarter.

The result compares with analysts' expectations of $1.31 billion in net profit on $13.89 billion in revenue, according to a Visible Alpha consensus.

Write to Pierre Bertrand at pierre.bertrand@wsj.com

data.title: Babcock International Raises Guidance as Strong Performance Continues
data.description:
(07:00 GMT) *Babcock Intl Raises FY View
(07:01 GMT) *Babcock Intl: Strong 1H Trading Performance Continued Throughout 3Q
(07:01 GMT) *Babcock Intl Performance in Jan Is Also Encouraging
(07:01 GMT) *Babcock Intl Sees FY25 Revenue of GBP4.9B
(07:02 GMT) *Babcock Intl Sees Double-digit Organic Growth in Nuclear, Strong Growth in Marine
(07:06 GMT) *Babcock Intl Expects FY25 Revenue to Exceed Top End of Range of Analyst Views
(07:06 GMT) *Babcock Intl Expects FY25 Underlying Op Profit to Exceed Top End of Range of Analyst Views
(07:07 GMT) *Babcock Intl: FY25 Market Revenue Range is GBP4.51B-GBP4.78B
(07:07 GMT) *Babcock Intl: FY25 Underlying Operating Profit Range is GBP327.1M-GBP339.7M
(07:29 GMT) Babcock International Raises Guidance as Strong Performance Continues

By Ian Walker

U.K. defense company Babcock International Group raised its full-year guidance after a strong performance over its fiscal year to date.

The company--a major contractor for the U.K. government--said Thursday that it now expects revenue and underlying operating profit to exceed the top end of analysts' expectations, with revenue of 4.9 billion pounds ($6.13 billion) for the year ending March 31.

The company provided a revenue consensus of 4.67 billion pounds, with a range of 4.51 billion to 4.78 billion pounds and underlying operating profit of 333.5 million pounds, with a range of 327.1 million to 339.7 million pounds.

It had previously guided for revenue to reach 4.56 billion pounds, with operating profit of 332 million pounds.

Babcock said that most of the expected revenue for the year is now under contract, having recently won a number of contracts. It expects double-digit organic growth in nuclear and strong growth in marine.

Last month the company said that it had won a 17-year contract to provide military air training to French pilots worth 795 million euros ($827 million).

The company's order backlog as at Sept. 30 stood at 9.5 billion pounds.

Write to Ian Walker at ian.walker@wsj.com

data.title: HCL Technologies Opens EV Charging R&D Center with ChargePoint
data.description:
HCL Technologies is partnering with ChargePoint, a network charging solutions provider for electric vehicles, to boost innovation in EV charging software, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

Under the partnership, HCLTech established an advanced research and development center for ChargePoint in Bengaluru. It will be a hub for ChargePoint's software development efforts in India.

Shares of the company were up nearly 1% in recent trade.

data.title: TBS Holdings Sees FY Net Y40.60B
TBS Holdings 9-Mos Net Y43.74B Vs Net Y38.32B
Kering: New Gucci Artistic Direction Will Be Announced in Due Time
Ningbo Cixing Scraps Plan to Buy Majority Stake in Wuhan Minsheng New Tech
data.description:
Ningbo Cixing decided to terminate plans to buy a controlling stake in Wuhan Minsheng New Technology through the issuance of shares, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange filing on Thursday.

The Chinese intelligent knitting machinery manufacturer made the decision after the parties failed to reach an agreement, the filing said.

The company's shares dropped 13% at the close.

data.title: Mrs. Bectors Food Clocks Decline in Fiscal Q3 Consolidated Net Profit
data.description:
Mrs. Bectors Food Specialities' consolidated net profit slipped to 345.9 million Indian rupees in the fiscal third quarter from 346.1 million rupees a year ago, according to an Indian bourse filing on Wednesday by the cookies and crackers manufacturer.

Earnings per share during the quarter ended Dec. 31, 2024, dropped to 5.64 rupees from 5.89 rupees a year earlier.

Revenue from operations in fiscal Q3 increased to 4.92 billion rupees from 4.29 billion rupees a year ago.

The company also declared an interim dividend of 3 rupees per share, according to the filing.

data.title: Securitas AB Full Year Report 2024 | January-December
data.description:
Securitas AB Full Year Report 2024 | January-December

PR Newswire

STOCKHOLM, Feb. 6, 2025

STOCKHOLM, Feb. 6, 2025 /PRNewswire/ --

October--December 2024

  • Total sales MSEK 41 794 (39 542)
  • Organic sales growth 4 percent (6)
  • Real sales growth within technology and solutions 6 percent (6)
  • Operating income before amortization MSEK 3 036 (2 683)
  • Operating margin 7.3 percent (6.8)
  • Earnings per share, SEK 2.86 (2.11)
  • Earnings per share before IAC, SEK 3.05 (2.44)
  • Cash flow from operating activities 153 percent (166)

January--December 2024

  • Total sales MSEK 161 921 (157 249)
  • Organic sales growth 5 percent (9)
  • Real sales growth within technology and solutions 6 percent (34)
  • Operating income before amortization MSEK 11 200 (10 247)
  • Operating margin 6.9 percent (6.5)
  • Earnings per share, SEK 9.01 (2.24)
  • Earnings per share before IAC, SEK 10.81 (9.59)
  • Net debt/EBITDA ratio 2.5 (2.7)
  • Cash flow from operating activities 84 percent (80)
  • Proposed dividend for 2024 of SEK 4.50 (3.80) per share, distributed in two equal installments

Comments from the President and CEO

"Strong strategic execution and performance in 2024"

"We concluded 2024 with an operating margin of 7.3 perSHYcent (6.8) in the fourth quarter. The improvement was mainly driven by the security services business in Europe. Security services in North America and our global technology and solutions business also supported.

Organic sales growth was 4 percent in the fourth quarter. Real sales growth in our technology and solutions business was 6 percent, supporting the shift in the mix toward higher-margin business.

Operating cash flow was 153 percent (166) in the fourth quarter and 84 perSHYcent (80) for the full year. Cash flow generation in 2024 supported an accelerated deleveraging to a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 2.5 (2.7) at year-end.

SHAPING SECURITAS FOR LONG-TERM SUSTAINABLE SHAREHOLDER VALUE

In a time of heightened global uncertainty and an increased threat environment, clients are looking for a future-oriented partner with extensive security expertise. Our long-term partnership approach, combined with our global presence, technology and digital capabilities is a critical differentiator that makes us the partner of choice.

Our business model is resilient, and we drive operational value creation through growth in technology and solutions, improved portfolio profitability in security services, cost efficiency and digital innovation. In the fourth quarter, our margin on new sales improved thanks to a strengthened client offering. Additionally, active portfolio manageSHYment contributed to improved profitabilSHYity in our security services business, especially in Europe.

Our technology and solutions business delivered healthy growth and operating margin improvement in the quarter. By the end of the year, we closed the STANLEY Security integration program, and I am pleased with how we have successfully managed this large and complex process. With a strong value proposition in place and the integration now behind us, we are in a good position to focus on operational delivery and client engagement.

After a few years of extensive work under our transformation programs to create a modern and more digital Securitas, we have now completed yet another milestone with the finalization of the Ibero-American program. In Europe, we still have some work remaining and will continue our implementation efforts in 2025 and 2026, albeit at continued lower investment levels.

As we build the new Securitas, we continue to create scale and increased automation opportunities. In January 2025, we started to execute additional identified opportunities to run our business at a structurally lower cost level which will deliver MSEK 200 in annualized savings by the end of 2025, primarily in Europe. The total cost of this business optimization program is MSEK 225, and will be accounted for as an item affecting comparability in 2025. Total investments related to items affecting comparability will be materially reduced in 2025 to approximately MSEK 375.

As part of our strategy, we are continuing to assess our business mix and presence to enhance our performance and long-term competitive position. Late in the fourth quarter, we signed a put option agreement to divest our airport security business in France due to the limited opportunity to pursue our long-term strategy at a healthy financial performance.

2024 — A STRONG YEAR

As I look back at 2024, I am proud of the Securitas team and our perSHYformance. We have taken important steps on our journey to transform Securitas into the leading intelligent security partner, strengthened our operating margin to 6.9 percent (6.5) and improved earnings per share by 15 percent in 2024. We still have important work ahead of us, but we are on track with our strategic plan, and we remain committed to achieving our target of an 8 percent operating margin by the end of 2025."

Magnus Ahlqvist

President and CEO

PRESENTATION OF THE FULL YEAR REPORT

Analysts and media are invited to participate in a telephone SHYconference on February 6, 2025, at 9.30 a.m. (CET) where President and CEO Magnus Ahlqvist and CFO Andreas Lindback will present the report and answer questions. The SHYtelephone conference will also be audio cast live via Securitas' website www.securitas.com

To follow the audio cast of the telephone conference via the web, please follow the link

www.securitas.com/en/investors/financial-reports-and-presentations/

A recorded version of the audio cast will be available at

www.securitas.com/en/investors/financial-reports-and-presentations/ after the SHYtelephone conference.

For further information, please contact:

Micaela Sjökvist, Vice President, Investor Relations +46 76 116 7443

ABOUT SECURITAS

Securitas is a world-leading safety and security solutions partner that helps make your world a safer place. Nine decades of deep experience means we see what others miss. By leveraging technology in partnership with our clients, SHYcombined with an innovative, holistic approach, we're transforming the security SHYindustry. With approximately 336 000 employees in 44 markets, we see a SHYdifferent world and SHYcreate sustainable value for our clients by protecting what matters most — their people and assets.

Group financial targets

Securitas has four financial targets:

  • 8--10 percent technology and solutions annual average real sales growth
  • 8 percent Group operating margin by year-end 2025, with a >10 percent SHYlong-term operating margin ambition
  • A net debt to EBITDA ratio below 3.0x
  • An operating cash flow of 70--80 percent of operating income before SHYamortization

data.title: Accrelist Unit, Mclean Technologies Extend Conditional Period for Proposed Disposal of Plastic Business
data.description:
Accrelit's subsidiary, We Total Engineering, and Mclean Technologies mutually agreed to extend the conditional period for the proposal disposal of We Total Engineering's plastic business to the latter for from Feb. 7 to March 7.

All other terms of the agreement remain unchanged, a filing with the Singapore Exchange said on Wednesday.

Earlier, We Total Engineering had entered into an agreement with Mclean Technologies for the disposal of its plastic business for around 6.0 million ringgit, according to an earlier filing on Oct. 8.

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0222 ET - The FTSE 100 is expected to open 73 points higher, or 0.9%, according to IG. The index closed 52 points higher, or 0.6%, to 8523 points Wednesday. Market sentiment across Europe and the U.S. has improved on waning trade tensions with President Trump, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. Investors are also looking ahead to the latest policy decision from the Bank of England at 1200 GMT. The BOE is expected to lower interest rates by 25 basis points as U.K. Treasury chief Rachel Reeves's growth plans are "clouded by the country's restricted finances and the economy calls for BOE support," she says.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1829 ET - Ford would be able to manage a few weeks of President Trump's proposed tariffs given the rate and flow of its products, CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts. If they persisted, though, they would have a devastating impact, he says. The company already has plans to expand some of its U.S. plants to mitigate higher import costs, but if the proposed tariffs become a long-term reality, Ford would have to make some major strategy shifts, such as building all new plants in the U.S., Farley says. Shares fall 5.1% after hours. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1817 ET - Ares Management expects that some of the policies of the new Trump administration will foster dealmaking, giving a further boost to what the private-credit fund manager sees as an already favorable market for investments, Ares CEO Michael Arougheti says during an earnings call. "We expect transaction activity to expand year-over-year," he says. "The new administration could provide some tailwinds for deployment, especially if an improved regulatory and antitrust environment enables more business growth and [mergers and acquisitions] activity in the market." Ares ended December with $133 billion in dry powder, or capital available to spend, a record for the firm. (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

1814 ET - Ford CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts that President Trump's proposed tariffs could hurt the U.S. auto industry. "There's no question that tariffs at the 25% level from Canada and Mexico, if they're protracted, would have a huge impact on our industry, with billions of dollars of industry profits wiped out and adverse effects on U.S. jobs," he says. Consumers would also face higher prices, he adds. Ford applauds Trump's administration for the agreements with Mexico and Canada earlier this week and says it is closely monitoring the situation in China. Shares fall 5.3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1811 ET - Canadian Trade Minister Mary Ng says Ottawa is ready to enter talks with Trump administration officials on "priority" bilateral trade issues with a goal of avoiding a 25% tariff in early March. Ng provides no concrete examples on what the Liberal government might do, during an interview with Canada's CTV News. Ng, though, says Ottawa won't make concessions on agriculture, especially measures aimed at protecting Canada's dairy producers. Canada's system of protecting dairy farmers was a point of attack for President Trump's nominee for Commerce Secretary, Howard Lutnick, during a confirmation hearing last week. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1808 ET - Ford CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts that the company believes President Trump and his administration, as well as other congressional leaders, are committed to strengthening the U.S. auto industry. "That is certainly our expectation, and we look forward to working with our leaders to make sure that that becomes a reality, because they understand and appreciate how vital our industry is to jobs, the economy, our national security and the communities across our country," he says. Shares fall 5.3% after the company says it expects profitability to shrink in 2025. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1458 ET - Investors grew worried about inflation during the trade war flare-up, but the U.S. housing market is likely to counter price pressures elsewhere, Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He expects new-home supply to increase, cooling prices and helping keep the Fed on track to cut rates. Housing market dynamics also make investing in mortgages an attractive option for fixed-income investors. Triick is particularly bullish on higher-yielding mortgages not backed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. "You are getting elevated yields and the fundamentals of housing are still really strong," he says. A drop on house prices could increase defaults, but Triick says it is unlikely to happen. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1454 ET - First-term Trump tariffs didn't matter much for aggregate inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says, speaking at a conference in Detroit. But he warns the impact could be worse this time around. As companies adapted their supply chains, they likely shifted "all the easiest things out of China," Goolsbee says, leaving behind production inputs that are harder to move. Such trade will remain vulnerable to tariffs because there are no ready substitutes, Goolsbee says: "Tariffs on intermediate goods raise the costs of production for domestic manufacturers of other goods, so the inflation impact is unlikely to stay only in the directly affected products." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1447 ET - Tariffs would likely complicate the Fed's job in responding to further stubbornness in inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says. Speaking at a conference in Detroit, Goolsbee says that depending on White House policy, the Fed will have to determine whether tariff-induced price increases are a one-time adjustment or reflect a deeper pattern before reacting. "If we see inflation rising or progress stalling in 2025, the Fed will be in the difficult position of trying to figure out if the inflation is coming from overheating or if it's coming from tariffs," Goolsbee says. "That distinction will be critical for deciding when or even if the Fed should act." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1445 ET - A third large weekly inventory draw leaves U.S. distillate stocks 12% below their five-year average as a colder-than-expected January drove extra demand for heating fuel. The inventory decline "is not too surprising given where refineries are operating, still below 85% of operable capacity, but that is something that creates near-term price risk for diesel," says Matt Muenster, chief economist at transport technology firm Breakthrough. Monday's pause in threatened tariffs on Canadian oil took some pressure off prices, and although the cost would likely be shared among Canadian oil producers, U.S. refiners and consumers, "if they had stayed, that ultimately was going to get passed to transportation pretty quickly," he adds. Also offsetting the price impact of lower diesel stocks is the seasonal build in crude oil inventories, Muenster says. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1431 ET - Front-month gold futures settle again at a new record, with that contract closing up 0.6% to $2,871.60 a troy ounce. It's the third consecutive session that gold climbed, and the sixth positive close in the past seven sessions. Amid confusion surrounding U.S. tariffs and retaliation, physical gold demand is strong, says Phil Flynn of Price Futures Group. "We're starting to see huge demand for physical gold and reports that the Comex exchange is having to secure more supplies to satisfy massive gold deliveries," says Flynn in a note. SPDR Gold shares are also higher in late trading, with shares up 0.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1332 ET - Stanley Black & Decker is making $150 million in targeted investments this year aimed at accelerating its supply chain overhaul to reduce the company's reliance on production in China, CFO Patrick Hallinan says on an analyst call. The tool maker anticipates that Donald Trump's targeted tariffs on China could cost the company $90 million to $100 million in 2025 if they remain in place, though Stanley believes it could get the impact down to $10 million to $20 million with higher prices and further changes to its supply chain, the CFO says. Stanley is waiting for greater clarity on tariffs before enacting any new measures to battle the cost inflation, the CFO says. "We expect the current situation to remain dynamic," he says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: FTSE 100 Seen Opening Higher Ahead of BOE Decision — Market Talk
data.description:
0722 GMT - The FTSE 100 is expected to open 73 points higher, or 0.9%, according to IG. The index closed 52 points higher, or 0.6%, to 8523 points Wednesday. Market sentiment across Europe and the U.S. has improved on waning trade tensions with President Trump, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. Investors are also looking ahead to the latest policy decision from the Bank of England at 1200 GMT. The BOE is expected to lower interest rates by 25 basis points as U.K. Treasury chief Rachel Reeves's growth plans are "clouded by the country's restricted finances and the economy calls for BOE support," she says.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0714 GMT - The Philippine central bank is likely to cut its benchmark overnight reverse-repurchase rate by 25 bps to 5.50% next week, says HSBC. December and January CPI readings surprised on the upside but remains well within the lower-bound range of the BSP's 2%-4% target band, economist Aris Dacanay writes in a note. The central bank loosening its monetary policy will lend some much needed support to the economy after disappointing 4Q economic growth, he says. HSBC continues to expect the BSP to cut its policy rate by a total of 75 bps this year to 5.00%, while moving back its forecasts for the ending of the cycle to 4Q from 3Q. HSBC also projects the policy rate will remain constant at 5.00% throughout 2026. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0713 GMT - German Bunds may struggle initially as markets digest heavy bond issuance from eurozone peers Spain and France, say Commerzbank Research's Hauke Siemssen and Erik Liem. However, the Bank of England's anticipated interest-rate cut and below-consensus U.S. ISM data should keep the overall bond-positive sentiment intact, the rates strategists say. "Overall, we expect today's duration heavy supply to weigh on markets in the morning but relief should be in store once the auctions have been absorbed," they say. France will auction 11 billion euros-13 billion euros in long-dated bonds, while Spain will sell 5.75 billion euros-7.25 billion euros in bonds across short- to ultra-long maturities, as well as in an inflation-linked bond. The 10-year Bund rises 1.5 basis points to 2.373%, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0713 GMT - The risk of a one-off rate cut by the Bank of Thailand after 1Q is increasing given the low inflation and headwinds to growth, says Aris Dacanay, economist at HSBC Global Research. The government aims to lower electricity tariffs this year, he says in a note. Also, with trade tariffs being raised between the U.S. and China, Chinese exports may be rerouted to Thailand, increasing competition among manufacturers and putting a ceiling on their ability to raise prices. This is also a headwind to Thai GDP if consumers substitute local goods for goods produced in China, he says. While a rate cut in 2025 is not his baseline scenario, Dacanay says the headwinds will likely keep the BOT on its toes in the quarters ahead. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0703 GMT - Former Bank of Japan Gov. Haruhiko Kuroda endorses the central bank's interest-rate increases and other policy changes made since March 2024, which reverse most of his aggressive monetary easing programs. "It is only natural that the BOJ is normalizing monetary policy," given the economic recovery, price rises and wage growth, Kuroda says at an event in Tokyo. Kuroda plays down criticism among some economists that the BOJ's rate increases are ruining the virtuous cycle of income, spending and inflation. Wages don't have to keep increasing at 5% like last year, the former governor says, adding that the BOJ could achieve sustainable, stable 2% inflation as long as wages grow by around 3%. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield has only a little more room to fall, ING's rates strategists say in a note. The strategists have been tactically bullish--or positive in the short term--on U.S. Treasurys in the past couple of weeks, so the recent fall in yields is moving in their short-term direction, they say. "Another 10-20 basis points and it will begin to look like its gone too far, and that's where the backup starts," they say. The 10-year Bund yield rises 1 basis point to 4.432%, according to Tradeweb. The 10-year Treasury yield fell 9 basis points on Wednesday, helped by weaker-than-expected ISM survey data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0642 GMT - Philippines' hotter-than-expected January inflation print is unlikely to dissuade the central bank from cutting the policy rate by 25 bps next week, but an April rate cut could be at risk if inflation remains stubbornly high in February or March, BofA Global Research says. The monthly rates of price growth have been above average since November 2024, partly due to an unusual spate of bad weather and natural calamities that affected food supplies, says research analyst Jojo Gonzales. Food inflation accelerated to 3.8% on year and 1.1% on month in January from 3.4% on year and 0.8% on month in December, he notes, adding that rice prices haven't eased despite lower import tariffs. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0523 GMT - Australia's economic activity has cooled, but larger states such as Victoria have witnessed a sharper slowdown, while smaller states like Western Australia have been more resilient, economists at HSBC Global Research say. With fiscal challenges, pandemic policy and postpandemic behavioral changes affecting migration patterns and housing markets, smaller states will likely continue to outperform in 2025, they say. Affordability in smaller states has attracted people, companies and public agencies allowing more flexible working arrangements, they note. While Victoria's public debt has risen sharply, making it one of the most indebted state governments in the world outside the U.S., Western Australia has benefited from strong activity, high commodity prices, and favorable GST arrangements, they add. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0505 GMT - The suspension of a U.S. trade provision allowing goods valued under $800 to enter the country duty-free will likely have an immediate negative impact on Chinese exporters, Nomura analysts say in a research note. Without the de minimis exemption, direct-to-consumer exporters would need to obtain approvals from various regulatory agencies and be subject to higher tariffs, the analysts say. Nomura estimates that 10% of Chinese shipments would no longer be able to enter the U.S. as a result. The analysts expect consumer demand to roughly halve, correlating with an expected 55% price hike. Meanwhile, they estimate that Chinese goods entering the U.S. under the de minimis exemption could plummet 60%. Therefore, ending the de minimis provision could shave off 1.3 percentage points from China's export growth and 0.2 percentage point from GDP growth. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0441 GMT - India's Central Bank Is Tipped to Cut Its Policy Repo Rate by 25 Bps to 6.25% on Friday, According to All 12 Economists Polled by The Wall Street Journal. The government's focus on narrowing the fiscal deficit, and high frequency indicators pointing to a growth slowdown are key factors supporting expectations of a rate cut, DBS Group Research's Radhika Rao says in a note. With inflation falling, room for rate cuts and easier liquidity has opened up, HSBC economists say. Citi Research analysts expect a 25bp cut, highlighting that this has been the longest period the RBI has been on hold after the end of a rate hike cycle. However, Citi notes some investors still expect the RBI to stay on hold due to global uncertainties. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0433 GMT - China's retail sales growth may slow after the Lunar New Year holiday break, say Citi analysts in a research note. Sales of major retailer and catering companies during the Lunar New Year grew 4.1%, the analysts say, citing official data. Retail sales after the holiday period is likely to decelerate more dramatically, compared with the period before and during the Lunar New Year, where heavy promotions were implemented. The retail sales over the period January to mid-February would be more meaningful for investors to gauge the underlying consumption recovery than the short-term sales performance during the Lunar New Year break, they add. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0429 GMT - The U.S. dollar is likely to stay under pressure versus the yen, based on charts, says Quek Ser Leang at UOB's Global Economics & Markets Research in a research report. The currency pair's latest price movements have resulted in a swift build-up in weekly downward momentum, the markets strategist notes. The currency pair breaking below the bottom of the weekly Ichimoku cloud at 151.10 is likely to trigger further sharp losses, the strategist adds. Subsequent meaningful support level would be at December 2024's low of 148.63. USD/JPY is 0.3% lower at 152.20. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Mitsubishi Chemical's Nine-Month Profit Plunges 43%
data.description:
Mitsubishi Chemical Group recorded a 43% decline in attributable net income for the nine months ended Dec. 31, 2024, to 59.4 billion yen from 103.9 billion yen in the year-ago period, a Thursday bourse filing said.

Earnings per share were 41.70 yen for the three quarters, down from 69.88 yen in the corresponding period of the last fiscal year.

The chemical product manufacturer's sales revenue rose 2.7% to 3.332 trillion yen in the nine months from 3.245 trillion yen a year prior.

The company expects an attributable net income of 52 billion yen, a basic EPS of 36.54 yen per share, a sales revenue of 4.470 trillion yen, and a total dividend of 32 yen per share for the fiscal year ending March 31.

Shares of the company were up over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Chinese Shares End Higher as Investors Await Tariff Developments — Market Talk
data.description: Chinese shares ended higher, with the Shanghai Composite Index rising 1.3% to 3270.66. The Shenzhen Composite Index added 2.3% to 1964.55 and the ChiNext Price Index gained 2.8% to 2120.75. Investors are focusing on developments regarding tariffs. Among major stocks, BYD shares rose 10% on high expectations for its upcoming smart car press conference. SMIC added 3.0% and Great Wall Motor gained 2.1%. Decliners included Beijing Kingsoft Office Software, which was 1.2% lower, and China Construction Bank, which was 0.5% lower. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)
